context:
I'm a 26-year-old software developer. I've taken an indefinite break from work to strengthen/explore myself, expand my interdisciplinary knowledge graph, and venture into solo projects. My design/architectonic ethos is to be lean, rapid, and iterative, so that I can touch and explore the widest possible set of solution spaces for whatever we are pursuing. We gotta be nimble and think outside the box to eliminate the ordinary clutter/overhead as much as possible, while crafting solid and reliable artifacts. We achieve this nimbleness by leveraging high-impact architectural/systemic decisions/actions before hitting diminishing returns, not by taking superficial trade-offs that degrade the clarity or solidity of our solutions.
I'm exploring cross-exchange Crypto Perp-Perp funding rate arbitrage. My goal is to actively hunt and rotate capital into localized, extreme funding rate anomalies (e.g.,500% - 1000%+ APY) while maintaining strict delta-neutrality.
I approach this as a discretionary "Cyborg" Arbitrageur. I manually filter out micro-caps to avoid low-liquidity manipulation. I manually check for structural volatility events (token unlocks, mainnet launches) before deploying. I use Zeek.tools as my primary radar/scanner, cross-referenced with Coinglass and Velo Data. I strictly trade USDT-margined perpetual futures on both legs (e.g., Long Binance / Short Bybit, Long KuCoin / Short OKX) to bypass spot borrow costs and liquidity issues. I strictly avoid Inverse/Coin-margined perps to prevent non-linear payoff drift (Quanto risk). I deploy 100% of allocated capital (no idle stables). I cap leverage at 1x to 2x for natural liquidation buffers (95% to 45% distance).
I accept the counterparty/exchange insolvency risks and recognize that this strategy has a scaling ceiling (slippage on larger capital sizes) which is irrelevant at my current $25k tier.
A custom, high-frequency, event-driven arbitrage system built with Python and CCXT Pro. Hosted on a Tokyo or Singapore VPS to achieve 5-15ms latency to exchange matching engines. The architecture strictly separates network-heavy preparation from microsecond execution via a unified stateful Engine and a lightweight IPC terminal.
A physical crash-recovery ledger mapping active pairs, base amounts, and assigned exchanges. Asynchronously written to by the Engine; acts strictly as a backup to the live RAM state. An OS-buffered, timestamped physical log recording execution parameters, API responses, and structural error events without blocking the event loop.
The monolithic state manager and execution processor. Maintains a local HTTP server (port 8080) to receive IPC commands, pre-loads exchange market rules into RAM, and holds persistent REST keep-alive and WebSocket streams to eliminate network round-trip bottlenecks.
Performs 0ms pre-flight validation against RAM-cached limits and balances. If a dual-leg execution fractures, an automated inverse rollback is instantly fired on the surviving leg to guarantee absolute delta-neutrality.
Background asyncio tasks running within the same event loop. Monitors margin ratios and position states. Executes instant unhedged-leg rollbacks on liquidation events to prevent naked directional exposure. Polls predicted funding rates to trigger preemptive dual-leg exits prior to unfavorable payout cycles.
A lightweight, stateless command-line interface acting as a "dumb terminal" to pass human-driven routing signals to the Core Engine.
Accepts target symbol, exchanges, and leverage. Triggers the Engine to authenticate, download market rules to RAM, set leverage, and establish keep-alive connections prior to any execution pressure. Accepts target symbol, exchanges, and exact base token amount. Triggers the Engine to fire concurrent REST market orders and map the position into RAM. Accepts target symbol. Triggers the Engine to lookup active exchanges and base token amounts from RAM, execute synchronized reduce-only flattening orders, and clear the state.
